notes: 54-card library brainstormed 2026-07-12 from PRD 026 domains plus counselor themes (NACAC fit framework, Gallup 'Big Six' experiences, Your College-Bound Kid list-building criteria).

Preference keys are unipolar qualities (e.g. scale.large and scale.small are distinct keys, both phrased as positive experiences). Opposing qualities are reconciled by evidence matchers in discovery_policy_v1, not by same-key conflict detection; same-key seek/avoid conflicts arise from reactions and edits per PRD 026 §3.

Evidence keys are declarative source.field pointers, provisional until discovery_policy_v1 binds matchers. cds.* keys depend on corpus extraction coverage — the Milestone 0 data spike must report per-card coverage before any 'data' card is trusted in a recommendation reason.
Protected-status preference cards (LGBTQ+ climate, disability support, campus safety) were considered and deferred past v1 by owner decision 2026-07-12.
The opening deck is decks/opening-v1.json (approved, revision 2), drawn from this library.

2026-07-12 spike amendment: early-research upgraded reflection_only -> data (v2). Undergraduate Research entered CDS Section E1 in the 2022-23 schema (schemas/cds_schema_2021_22-to-2022_23.diff.md) and 245 schools have E.117 extracted for 2024-25. Counts: 25 data / 12 proxy / 17 reflection-only.

2026-07-12 review amendment: evidence keys corrected to name real columns (scorecard.locale, scorecard.median_debt_completers, scorecard.earnings_10yr_median, scorecard.net_price_0_30k, scorecard.graduation_rate_4yr/6yr); four-year-finish (v2) no longer cites six-year completion. ipeds.ic.* keys (religious_affiliation, athletic_division on faith-life and big-game-days) reference IPEDS IC attributes NOT YET LOADED into the serving layer — those cards cannot generate recommendation reasons until that load ships.

limitations.lim.reflection_only: No verified data source supports this yet, so it shapes your reflection and preference ledger but never explains why a school appears.
limitations.lim.e1_offering: The school reports offering this option in its Common Data Set; that says nothing about how many students use it or how strong it is.
limitations.lim.cip_breadth: Program breadth comes from degrees recently awarded (IPEDS); it shows academic range, not advising quality or how easy switching actually is.
limitations.lim.class_size: Class-size counts come from the school's CDS Section I and describe section sizes for one year, not teaching quality.
limitations.lim.size_proxy: School size and student-faculty ratio are rough proxies for personal attention; small numbers don't guarantee close relationships.
limitations.lim.locale_proxy: Federal locale codes describe the setting's category (city, town, rural), not how walkable or lively it actually feels.
limitations.lim.metro_distance: Distance is approximate straight-line miles, not travel time.
limitations.lim.housing_share: The share of students in college housing comes from the school's CDS for one year; it approximates campus life but can't measure atmosphere.
limitations.lim.participation_share: Participation shares come from the school's CDS for one year; they describe scale, not whether you'd feel at home.
limitations.lim.activities_checklist: The CDS activities checklist shows something exists; it can't measure how active or welcoming it is.
limitations.lim.composition: Enrollment composition uses federal reporting categories from a specific year; the categories are imperfect and don't capture lived campus experience.
limitations.lim.pell_proxy: Pell-share is a federal proxy from a specific year; how supported students actually feel is not measurable from this data.
limitations.lim.aid_no_prediction: Aid and price figures describe reported patterns for past students in a named year; they are never a prediction of your award or your family's price.
limitations.lim.outcomes_cohort: Retention, graduation, and earnings figures describe past cohorts, often several years old, and vary by program and student path.
limitations.lim.division_proxy: Athletic division indicates the scale of the athletics program, not the actual game-day culture.
limitations.lim.affiliation: Religious affiliation is a reported institutional attribute; campuses vary widely in how present faith life feels.

cards.statement: I can try classes in a few different subjects before committing to a major.
cards.explanation: Schools with many related programs give you real room to explore before you declare.

cards.statement: If I change my mind about my major, I won't have to transfer.
cards.explanation: When a school awards degrees across several related fields, changing direction usually means changing departments, not schools.

cards.statement: I can combine two interests into one degree — double major, or design my own.
cards.explanation: Some schools formally support double majors and student-designed majors.

cards.statement: There's an honors path with smaller, deeper classes if I want the challenge.
cards.explanation: Honors programs offer a more intense academic track inside a larger school.

cards.statement: I can take classes at nearby colleges too, not just my own.
cards.explanation: Cross-registration agreements let you enroll in courses at partner schools.

cards.statement: I can speed up — graduate early or start advanced work as an undergrad.
cards.explanation: Accelerated programs compress the path to a degree or blend into graduate work.

cards.statement: Most of my classes feel like conversations, not lectures in a giant hall.
cards.explanation: Schools report how many class sections are small, medium, and large.

cards.statement: My professors will actually know my name.
cards.explanation: Smaller schools and lower student-faculty ratios make personal attention more likely — but they're only proxies.

cards.statement: Going to office hours and getting real help is normal here.
cards.explanation: Whether students actually use office hours is a culture question no dataset answers yet.

cards.statement: Professors — not teaching assistants — teach most of my courses.
cards.explanation: Public data counts faculty but doesn't say who leads your actual class sessions.

cards.statement: There are professors here working on problems I care about.
cards.explanation: Matching faculty research to your interests takes department-level digging the data can't do yet.

cards.statement: I can get course credit for internships.
cards.explanation: Schools report whether internships can count toward your degree.

cards.statement: I could alternate semesters of work and school and graduate with a résumé.
cards.explanation: Cooperative education formally weaves paid work terms into the degree.

cards.statement: Studying abroad is normal here, not a rare exception.
cards.explanation: Schools report offering study abroad; how many students actually go isn't in the data.

cards.statement: Undergraduates join real research early — not just seniors.
cards.explanation: Schools report offering undergraduate research among their CDS special study options (a checklist item since the 2022-23 CDS).

cards.statement: Classes get out of the classroom — fieldwork, labs, studios, building things.
cards.explanation: How much learning happens outside lecture halls is a curriculum-design question the data can't see.

cards.statement: Independent study is real — I can pursue a question no course covers.
cards.explanation: Schools report whether students can earn credit for self-directed projects.

cards.statement: I can pick up a teaching certificate along the way.
cards.explanation: Some schools let you earn state teaching certification alongside your major.

cards.statement: I can walk from campus into a real town — coffee, food, places to be.
cards.explanation: Federal locale codes hint at town-and-campus settings, but walkability itself isn't measured.

cards.statement: The city IS my campus — internships, music, food all around me.
cards.explanation: Schools in large cities put urban life at your doorstep.

cards.statement: A classic campus — green quads, old buildings, a place that feels like somewhere.
cards.explanation: Campus beauty and feel are real preferences, but no dataset scores them.

cards.statement: Mountains, trails, or water close enough for a normal weekend.
cards.explanation: Outdoor access could be derived from geography data later; it isn't loaded yet.

cards.statement: Warm most of the school year sounds right to me.
cards.explanation: Climate data isn't loaded yet, so this shapes reflection only.

cards.statement: I want real seasons — snow included.
cards.explanation: Climate data isn't loaded yet, so this shapes reflection only.

cards.statement: A quieter setting where campus is the center of life.
cards.explanation: Rural and town settings where most students live on campus tend to center life on the school itself.

cards.statement: I can get to a big city for a weekend without living in one.
cards.explanation: Distance from campus to the nearest large metro can be computed from coordinates.

cards.statement: An airport with real flight options is within reach.
cards.explanation: Airport proximity data isn't loaded yet, so this shapes reflection only.

cards.statement: Most students live on campus, so evenings and weekends have life.
cards.explanation: Schools report what share of students live in college housing.

cards.statement: Campus doesn't empty out on Friday afternoon.
cards.explanation: High on-campus housing and many students from far away make a weekend campus more likely — but it's a proxy.

cards.statement: Greek life is a big part of the social scene.
cards.explanation: Schools report what share of students join fraternities and sororities.

cards.statement: There's a real arts scene — ensembles, theater, galleries open to every major.
cards.explanation: Schools list arts activities in the CDS; the checklist shows existence, not vibrancy.

cards.statement: Club and intramural sports are easy to join at any level.
cards.explanation: Schools list intramural and club sports in the CDS activities checklist.

cards.statement: Housing is guaranteed beyond freshman year.
cards.explanation: Housing guarantees aren't reported in structured data yet.

cards.statement: I want the energy of a big school — hundreds of clubs, a huge catalog.
cards.explanation: Enrollment and program breadth show a school's scale directly.

cards.statement: I want a school small enough that I'm a person, not a number.
cards.explanation: Enrollment shows scale directly; whether small feels personal is up to the school.

cards.statement: I keep seeing the same people across classes and clubs — and I like that.
cards.explanation: Smaller communities overlap more by nature, but the feeling itself isn't measured.

cards.statement: Game days matter — big crowds, school colors, the whole thing.
cards.explanation: Athletic division hints at the scale of sports culture; the culture itself isn't measured.

cards.statement: Traditions matter here — rituals every student shares.
cards.explanation: Traditions are real and unmeasured; this shapes reflection only.

cards.statement: Faith or spiritual life has a real home on campus.
cards.explanation: Religious affiliation is reported federally; how present faith life feels varies widely.

cards.statement: People collaborate here — it's supportive, not cutthroat.
cards.explanation: Academic culture is one of the most-asked-about qualities and one no dataset measures.

cards.statement: Service and volunteering are woven into ordinary student life.
cards.explanation: Service culture varies by campus and isn't captured in structured data.

cards.statement: Campus is politically engaged — debate and activism are part of the conversation.
cards.explanation: Civic energy is a genuine preference that sorts strongly both ways; no dataset measures it.

cards.statement: My classmates come from all over the country and the world.
cards.explanation: Schools report what share of students come from out of state and abroad.

cards.statement: I'll meet people whose backgrounds are genuinely different from mine.
cards.explanation: Enrollment composition is reported in federal categories with a stated year.

cards.statement: Being first in your family at college is common and supported here.
cards.explanation: Pell-share suggests how common first-generation and lower-income students are; support quality isn't measured.

cards.statement: The sticker price isn't the real price — I want schools where aid is worth investigating.
cards.explanation: Reported net prices by family income show where aid meaningfully lowers the real cost.

cards.statement: Merit scholarships can cut the cost even without financial need.
cards.explanation: Schools report non-need merit aid patterns — how many students get it and how much.

cards.statement: I want to graduate without heavy loans.
cards.explanation: Median debt and borrowing rates show the debt picture for past students.

cards.statement: Working a campus job to help pay my way is realistic here.
cards.explanation: Campus job availability isn't in structured data yet.

cards.statement: Almost everyone comes back for sophomore year — people stay because it works.
cards.explanation: First-year retention is reported by the school and federally.

cards.statement: Most students finish in four years, not five or six.
cards.explanation: Graduation rates show how reliably students actually finish on time.

cards.statement: There's a visible track record of graduates moving into careers.
cards.explanation: Federal earnings data exists for past cohorts; it's evidence to inspect, never a promise of a good job.

cards.statement: Career help starts early, not senior year.
cards.explanation: Career services quality and timing aren't in structured data.

cards.statement: Alumni actually answer when students reach out.
cards.explanation: Alumni-network warmth is real and unmeasured; this shapes reflection only.
